metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `src/rebar/_build_info.py` will be included in the sdist â€” either by adding it to `artifacts` under `[tool.hatch.build.targets.sdist]` in `pyproject.toml`, or by verifying that hatchling's build hook output is automatically captured by the `include = ["src/rebar"]` glob regardless of `.gitignore` status (document this explicitly in the plan).
findings.finding: The plan specifies `_build_info.py` is git-ignored (correct) and generated by a hatchling `BuildHookInterface` at build time, but does not address sdist artifact inclusion. The existing `pyproject.toml` uses `artifacts = [...]` under `[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]` to force-include other git-ignored generated files (e.g. `_engine/**/*`, `editor_assets/dist/**/*`). The `[tool.hatch.build.targets.sdist]` section has no `artifacts` key. Hatchling's default sdist file-discovery respects `.gitignore`, so a git-ignored generated file written by the hook may be silently dropped from the sdist unless `artifacts` (or `force-include`) is also declared for the sdist target â€” leaving sdist-based installs without the baked SHA, silently falling through to the omit-last path.
findings.location: AC3 â€” 'A hatchling build hook (BuildHookInterface) captures the commit SHA at wheel/sdist build into a generated, git-ignored src/rebar/_build_info.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `artifacts = ["src/rebar/_build_info.py"]` under `[tool.hatch.build.targets.sdist]` in `pyproject.toml`, mirroring the pattern already used for the wheel target. Alternatively, if hatchling guarantees that hook-generated files are always included regardless of gitignore (verify in hatchling docs), document that assurance in the AC3 implementation note so the implementer doesn't add a redundant entry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact field name (e.g., `rebar_version`) under which the stamp is added to `verify_record`'s `base` dict (mirroring how `verified_at_sha` is extracted and named there), so both `verify_signature` callers and `show` use the same key.
findings.finding: `verify_signature` / `verify_record` returns a fixed-schema dict (`base` in signing.py lines 271-281); the plan says the stamp is 'returned by `verify_signature`' but never specifies the field name under which `rebar_version` is added to this dict, nor whether it is extracted into `base` (like `verified_at_sha`) or left to the raw `manifest` list for callers to parse.
findings.location: AC line 6 ('returned by `verify_signature`') / src/rebar/signing.py lines 271-281 (`base` dict in `verify_record`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC line 6: '`verify_signature` returns the stamp as `rebar_version` in the verdict dict, extracted via `manifest_rebar_version(manifest)` and added to the `base` dict in `verify_record`, mirroring the `verified_at_sha` pattern at signing.py:280.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback for the SHA-resolution call inside `_verdict_manifest`: the stamp line must be omitted (never raise) on any resolution failure, matching the 'omit-last' guarantee, and mirroring the existing `try/except â†’ material=None â†’ omit` pattern already in `_verdict_manifest`.
findings.finding: `_verdict_manifest` in `transition_close.py` builds its manifest inline (not via `build_manifest`) and the plan gives no protocol for how it integrates the SHA-resolution helper, nor what fallback applies if the helper raises during a close operation â€” unlike the `material` fingerprint which has an explicit `try/except â†’ None â†’ omit` pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm and reconcile the location of `_verdict_manifest`: verify whether it lives in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py` (as parent states) or `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:282` (as child 8840 states) and update the inconsistent reference.
findings.finding: Parent context states both `build_manifest` (plan-review) and `_verdict_manifest` (completion-verifier) live in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py`, but child 8840 locates `_verdict_manifest` at `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:282`. One location is wrong, and an implementor following the parent context would patch the wrong file for `_verdict_manifest`.
findings.suggested_fix: Run a code search for `def _verdict_manifest` to determine the canonical file, then update the parent context (or child 8840's What section) so both agree on the correct path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief architectural note (CLAUDE.md entry or inline doc comment in the build hook) capturing the rationale for the generated `_build_info.py` file and the live-first / baked-second / omit-last resolution contract.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel build-system pattern â€” a hatchling BuildHookInterface that generates a git-ignored `src/rebar/_build_info.py` at wheel/sdist build time â€” but does not mention updating any ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc to capture this architectural decision.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria â€” AC3 / 'What gets recorded' section (baked-second path)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to CLAUDE.md (or a docstring at the top of the build hook module) explaining: (1) why a generated file is used instead of hatch-vcs, (2) the three-tier resolution order, (3) that the file is intentionally git-ignored and regenerated at build time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace line-number references in child 8840 with symbol-name references (e.g. 'near the head_sha() function in signing.py' instead of 'line 337').
findings.finding: Child 8840 pins a specific line number (`signing.py` line 337 for `head_sha()`, `transition.py` line 282 for `_verdict_manifest`) as navigational anchors. These line numbers will silently become stale as the codebase evolves, potentially misdirecting implementors to wrong locations.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'line 337' to 'near def head_sha()' and remove the ':282' line-number pin, referencing 'def _verdict_manifest' by name instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC 6: specify the exact `verify_signature` return-dict key (e.g. `'rebar_version'`) and the `show` output line label so the contract is unambiguous.
findings.finding: AC 6 ('surfaced in `show` (attestation block) and returned by `verify_signature`') does not specify the field name or format that `verify_signature` returns the stamp under, nor what the `show` output line looks like â€” an executor must infer the output contract.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC 6: '`verify_signature` includes a `rebar_version` key in its returned dict (value is the full stamp string or `None`); `show` prints a `rebar-version:` line in the attestation block mirroring the manifest format.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan-body section (e.g. under 'What gets recorded' or a new 'Surfacing the stamp' subsection) describing what changes in `show`'s attestation block and what `verify_signature` returns/exposes for the new `rebar-version` stamp.
findings.finding: AC line 6 names `show` (attestation block) and `verify_signature` as deliverables that must surface the new stamp, but no section of the plan body (Context, What gets recorded, Success Criteria, Scope/non-goals) describes these changes, what they output, or how they parse the new line. These are orphan criteria with no covering description.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria â€” line 6: 'The stamp is surfaced in `show` (attestation block) and returned by `verify_signature`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reference `manifest_rebar_version()` in the plan body (e.g., in 'What gets recorded' or alongside the description of `manifest_regver()`), noting its module location and that it is used by `show`/`verify_signature` to consume the new stamp.
findings.finding: AC line 4 introduces `manifest_rebar_version()` as a new public parser function (mirroring `manifest_regver()`), but the plan body never describes this function â€” its location, its signature, or its relationship to the rest of the parsing infrastructure. It is covered only by its own AC line.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria â€” line 4: 'A `manifest_rebar_version()` parser (mirroring `manifest_regver()`) extracts the stamp; returns `None` for pre-stamp manifests.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to 'What gets recorded': 'A `manifest_rebar_version()` helper (in `attest.py`, parallel to `manifest_regver()`) parses the stamp from an existing manifest string, returning `None` for pre-stamp manifests; it is called by `show` and `verify_signature`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly acknowledge existing callers of `verify_signature` in the plan and confirm the return-value change is additive (e.g., 'returns a dict; new key is additive and backward-compatible') or note any callers that need updating.
findings.finding: AC6 directs that `verify_signature` be changed to surface the new stamp, but the plan does not acknowledge existing callers of `verify_signature` that may depend on the current return-value shape.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria â€” AC6: 'The stamp is surfaced in show (attestation block) and returned by verify_signature'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line under AC6 such as: 'verify_signature returns a dict; the new rebar_version key is purely additive. Confirm no caller performs strict key-set equality checks; update any that do.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence to Scope/Non-goals explaining concretely why `hatch-vcs` cannot satisfy AC3 (e.g., 'hatch-vcs requires VCS-driven versioning which conflicts with the static `version = "0.6.0"` in pyproject.toml and cannot be used in SHA-only mode without changing the version scheme'), or reconsider whether `hatch-vcs` covers the need.
findings.finding: The plan dismisses `hatch-vcs` as prior art in a single non-explanatory sentence ('do NOT switch to hatch-vcs â€” only add SHA capture') without justifying why `hatch-vcs` cannot satisfy the SHA-baking requirement. `hatch-vcs` is a first-class hatchling plugin that writes VCS provenance (including commit SHA) to a generated `_version.py` at build time â€” functionally identical to the proposed `_build_info.py` hook â€” and it also supports a 'raw-first-parent' format that omits the version bump. The plan introduces a bespoke `BuildHookInterface` (novel pattern for this codebase) without demonstrating what property it provides that `hatch-vcs` cannot.
findings.location: Scope / non-goals: 'Version stays static; do NOT switch to hatch-vcs â€” only add SHA capture'
findings.suggested_fix: If the reason is that `hatch-vcs` forces a VCS-managed version (overwriting `version = "0.6.0"`), state that explicitly. If `hatch-vcs` supports a 'commit-only' template without managing the version, consider using it and dropping the custom hook entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (or add to the plan) that `src/rebar/_build_info.py` must be listed in `[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel] artifacts` (mirroring the existing `editor_assets/dist/` entry) so the generated file survives into the wheel; alternatively, explain the hatchling hook mechanism that ensures this automatically.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that a git-ignored `src/rebar/_build_info.py` generated by the build hook will be included in the wheel for PyPI installs, but the existing codebase already demonstrates that git-ignored files in `src/rebar/` require an explicit `artifacts` entry to land in the wheel. The `pyproject.toml` `[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]` already force-includes `src/rebar/llm/workflow/editor_assets/dist/**/*` via `artifacts` for exactly this reason. Without adding `src/rebar/_build_info.py` to `artifacts`, the generated file may be silently excluded from the wheel, making the baked-second fallback silently absent on PyPI installs.
findings.location: AC3: 'A hatchling build hook (BuildHookInterface) captures the commit SHA at wheel/sdist build into a generated, git-ignored src/rebar/_build_info.py (PyPI/wheel install)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"src/rebar/_build_info.py"` to the `artifacts` list in `[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]` in `pyproject.toml`, consistent with the existing pattern for the editor assets bundle.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the required pyproject.toml build-hook registration (e.g. `[tool.hatch.build.hooks.custom]` with `path = "hatch_build.py"`) so the `BuildHookInterface` implementation is actually invoked during `hatch build` / `pip wheel` â€” without this the hook class exists but never runs.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a hatchling `BuildHookInterface` build hook that bakes the commit SHA into `src/rebar/_build_info.py` at wheel/sdist build time, but omits the required `pyproject.toml` registration step. A custom hatchling build hook is only invoked at build time when it is registered â€” typically via `[tool.hatch.build.hooks.custom]` pointing to a `hatch_build.py` module (or a named plugin entry-point). The current `pyproject.toml` has no `[tool.hatch.build.hooks.*]` section and no `hatch_build.py` exists in the repo. Without this registration, the hook class can be implemented correctly and still never execute, leaving `_build_info.py` absent from every wheel/sdist build â€” the baked-SHA path silently degrades to omit for all PyPI installs.
findings.location: AC line 3 / 'What gets recorded' (baked-second path) / pyproject.toml `[tool.hatch.build]` section
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to `pyproject.toml` under `[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]` (or as a sibling section):
```toml
[tool.hatch.build.hooks.custom]
path = "hatch_build.py"
```
and place the `BuildHookInterface` subclass in a `hatch_build.py` at the repo root (hatchling's default resolution path for `[tool.hatch.build.hooks.custom]`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the SHA-resolution helper is called inside `build_manifest` (making it accept/emit the stamp directly via an optional `rebar_version: str | None` parameter, keeping I/O out of the manifest builder) or is called by each signing call-site (`sign_plan_review`, `_verdict_manifest`) before passing the result as a parameter.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether the SHA-resolution helper is called *inside* `build_manifest` (adding subprocess I/O to a currently pure function) or *outside* it (resolved string passed as a new parameter), creating ambiguous placement of the live-git call relative to `build_manifest`'s purity contract.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `rebar_version: str | None = None` parameter to `build_manifest` (keeping the function I/O-free); call `_resolve_rebar_version()` in `sign_plan_review` and in `_verdict_manifest` before invoking the manifest builder, mirroring how `regver=registry_version(repo_root)` is resolved at the call site today.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly gate child f247's implementation on child 8840 being merged first, or designate a single implementation owner for `signing.py` and document the expected final resolver signature in both stories.
findings.finding: child f247 modifies the SHA-resolution function in `signing.py` that child 8840 creates, with no explicit merge-coordination note. Both children touch the same function body, creating a concrete merge-conflict risk when developed in parallel.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hard prerequisite in f247's scope: 'Must be implemented after 8840 is merged.' Also, consider defining the resolver's full signature (including the baked fallback stub) in 8840 itself so f247 only fills in the import, reducing conflict surface.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
